conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess this, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let me start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children I'm asking about - Alex, who's 6, and Blake, who's 16.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Now I need to understand a bit more about your situation and the children.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake? By this I mean, do they live with you and do you look after them?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't live with me. They both live elsewhere, but I do help support them financially.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. To better understand your situation:

**Who do Alex and Blake currently live with, and what is your relationship to them?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with their other parent, and Blake also lives with their other parent. I'm their mum, but we separated and the children stayed with my ex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining. This is an important factor in determining eligibility.

**Does the parent they live with (your ex) currently claim Child Benefit for Alex and Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not sure if my ex claims Child Benefit for them or not. I just know I help out with money for both of them each week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Let me ask a couple more questions to get a full picture.

**Do Alex and Blake ever stay with you, and if so, how often or for how long?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't stay with me. They live with their other parent full-time. I just send money to help with their expenses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have a clearer picture now. Let me ask one final question:

**Are Alex and Blake currently in any kind of care arrangement (such as local authority care), in hospital long-term, or are they living with their other parent in a regular family home?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in local authority care for a couple of months now. Blake is living at home with their other parent in a regular family situation.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Based on the information you've provided, I'm sorry to inform you that **you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake**.

**For Alex (age 6):**
Although Alex is under 16, they are currently in local authority care and have been for a couple of months. You can only get Child Benefit for a child in care in very limited circumstances - typically if the child is in care for 8 weeks or less, or if you have a specific arrangement with the local authority to continue receiving Child Benefit. Additionally, Alex does not live with you and you don't have day-to-day responsibility for them.

**For Blake (age 16):**
Blake lives full-time with their other parent and does not stay with you. To be eligible for Child Benefit, you must be responsible for the child, which means they must normally live with you. While you do provide financial support (which is commendable), this alone doesn't meet the requirement for Child Benefit eligibility.

The parent that Blake lives with may be eligible to claim Child Benefit if they are responsible for Blake's day-to-day care. If your ex is not currently claiming Child Benefit, they should consider applying if Blake meets the age criteria (under 16, or under 20 if in approved education or training).

I understand this may not be the answer you were hoping for, but I hope this clarifies the situation.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation

Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 6 years old (under 16) but is currently in local authority care and does not live with you. According to the rules, you can only get Child Benefit for a child in care in very limited circumstances - generally if the child is in care for 8 weeks or less, or if you have an arrangement with the local authority and continue to get Child Benefit. Since Alex has been in local authority care for a couple of months and there's no indication of such an arrangement, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. Additionally, you are not responsible for Alex in the sense required - Alex does not live with you and you do not look after them on a day-to-day basis.

Blake is 16 years old and lives full-time with their other parent (your ex). Blake does not live with you, does not stay with you, and you do not look after them on a day-to-day basis. To be eligible for Child Benefit, you must be responsible for the child, which means they must normally live with you. Since Blake lives with their other parent full-time and you only provide financial support, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. The parent Blake lives with would be the person who could potentially claim Child Benefit.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is the mother of two children, Alex (6) and Blake (16), but is separated from their other parent. Neither child lives with the claimant. Alex is in local authority care and has been for a couple of months, while Blake lives full-time with their other parent. The claimant provides financial support but does not have day-to-day responsibility for either child. Under Child Benefit rules, to be eligible you must be responsible for the child, which generally means they live with you. The claimant does not meet this requirement for either child, and therefore is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex or Blake.
